enriched_points.expanded_content: Representationalism posits that conscious experiences correspond to mental representations of the world, where sensory qualities are interpreted as intentional contents of these representations.
Higher-order theories assert that consciousness arises from higher-order representations, where a mental state becomes conscious when one is aware of it through introspection.
Cognitive theories focus on the role of cognitive processes in consciousness, emphasizing that awareness results from the integration of perceptual and cognitive information.
Representationalism effectively integrates consciousness within a materialistic framework, explaining sensory experiences as intentional states without resorting to immaterial entities.
Critics argue that representationalism struggles to account for qualitative aspects of consciousness, such as qualia, which seem resistant to purely representational explanations.
Higher-order theories provide a clear mechanism for awareness by linking consciousness to the awareness of one's mental states, facilitating introspective access.
These theories face challenges in explaining non-introspective experiences, where individuals may have conscious experiences without higher-order awareness.
Cognitive theories highlight the importance of cognitive processes, demonstrating how complex mental functions contribute to the richness of conscious experience.
Cognitive theories may overlook the subjective, qualitative nature of consciousness, reducing it to mere cognitive processing without addressing lived experiences.
